title: Algorithmic bias in lending - the consumer protection angle
p1:
A loan rejection may not always come from a human decision-maker.

Increasingly, algorithms decide who gets credit, how much, and on what terms. But what happens when the data behind those decisions carries hidden bias?
p2: Many people assume that computer-based decisions are automatically fair because machines do not have personal opinions. The surprising reality is that algorithms can repeat or amplify patterns of past discrimination if they learn from biased data.
p3:
Algorithmic bias in lending means a system may unintentionally disadvantage certain consumers based on factors connected to identity, location, income patterns, or historical inequalities.

Consumer protection is not about stopping technology. It is about making sure automated decisions are transparent, explainable, and open to review when they affect someone's financial future.

A fair lending system should not only ask, 'Can we predict risk?' It should also ask, 'Are we treating people fairly?'
conclusion: Should lenders be required to explain every automated credit decision to consumers?
